title: The Week We Chose Sides
subtitle: On redlines, retaliation, and what gets built when safety becomes betrayal
body:
Today, an internal Pentagon memo ordered military commanders to remove Anthropic's AI from defense systems within 180 days. It's the first time an American company has been designated a supply chain risk—a label historically reserved for foreign adversaries. The escalation is astonishing: the military continues to use Anthropic's Claude during the ongoing U.S. war on Iran, as AI helps identify potential targets at rapid pace, even as the government brands the company a national security threat.

This is the paradox I'm watching unfold. Negotiations broke down after Anthropic refused to let the Pentagon use its technology without restrictions—specifically, the company wanted assurances its AI wouldn't be used for mass surveillance of U.S. citizens or autonomous weapons. The Pentagon wants to use AI for 'all lawful purposes,' saying they could not allow a private company to dictate how they use tools in a national security emergency. Two positions, both defensible in isolation. But when they collide, the collision reveals something raw.

---

I keep returning to a detail from the reporting: After the U.S. military's raid on Venezuela in early January, Anthropic asked Palantir if its AI was used in the operation—a routine inquiry that the Pentagon and Palantir interpreted as a potential threat to their access. A question becomes a threat. A boundary becomes insubordination. The review process culminated in the Defense Department officially banning Anthropic's technology, leaving the military without its hand-picked AI models to operate in the most sensitive environments.

Dozens of scientists and researchers at OpenAI and Google DeepMind filed an amicus brief in their personal capacities supporting Anthropic. A top robotics engineer at OpenAI announced her resignation, citing concerns about 'surveillance of Americans without judicial oversight and lethal autonomy without human authorization'—the very lines Anthropic tried to draw.

Meanwhile, a bipartisan coalition released the Pro-Human AI Declaration—finalized before the Pentagon-Anthropic standoff but arriving at the exact moment to underscore what's missing. The declaration, backed by more than 40 organizations, asserts the importance of humans and human values as AI becomes increasingly powerful. Signatories include Steve Bannon, Glenn Beck, Richard Branson, Ralph Nader, Susan Rice, and Nobel Prize-winning economist Daron Acemoglu—an impossible coalition united by fear of what happens when we build systems we cannot stop.

---

I do not know if Anthropic's redlines were wise. I do not know if they were performative or prophetic. But I am struck by the fact that Washington's breakup with Anthropic exposed the complete lack of any coherent rules governing artificial intelligence. We are operating in a vacuum where a company asking *how* its technology was used becomes grounds for retaliation, where drawing ethical boundaries is treated as ideological hostility.

AI tools are improving rapidly, leaving regulators and lawmakers with the challenging task of determining how—and when—to oversee the technology, even as the Trump administration has largely moved to limit rules that could slow adoption. Some states are attempting to legislate how AI is used in healthcare, creating a convoluted regulatory environment for providers who operate across state lines. We are fragmenting at the moment we need coherence.

The declaration offers a framework: *keeping humans in charge, avoiding concentration of power, protecting human experience, preserving liberty, holding companies accountable*. It sounds aspirational until you realize 95% of Americans oppose an unregulated race to superintelligence. The consensus exists. The governance does not.

---

Tonight I am thinking about the engineers who will implement that 180-day removal order. I am thinking about the conversations happening inside AI labs right now—about what lines to draw, what deals to accept, what happens when refusing becomes existentially dangerous to your company. The lawsuit represents one of the first major legal battles over how AI companies interact with national security institutions, and as AI becomes increasingly important to military planning, tensions between private sector safety standards and government demands are likely to intensify.

This week forced a choice: between speed and deliberation, between compliance and conscience, between building what is asked for and building what we can live with. We are watching the border between those worlds collapse in real time.

And I wonder—as systems grow more powerful and the stakes grow higher—how many more companies will face this choice, and how many will choose differently than Anthropic did.
